revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

{{神社| 名称=山梨縣護國神社| 所在地=山梨県甲府市岩窪町608| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|01|N|138|34|56|E|}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 創建=明治12年（1879年）| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 例祭=[[4月5日]]・[[10月5日]]}}
'''山梨縣護國神社'''（やまなしけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の英霊25039柱を祀る。
[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。
現鎮座地は[[柳沢吉保]]の菩提寺・永慶寺の跡であり、近くには[[武田神社]]がある。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。
==外部リンク== *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/list/s_1062.html 山梨縣護國神社]（山梨県神社庁）

'''山梨縣護國神社'''（やまなしけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の英霊25039柱を祀る。
[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。
現鎮座地は[[柳沢吉保]]の菩提寺・永慶寺の跡であり、近くには[[武田神社]]がある。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。

[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。

{{神社| 名称=山梨縣護國神社| 画像=[[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|280px]]| 所在地=山梨県甲府市岩窪町608| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|01|N|138|34|56|E|}}| 祭神=護国の[[英霊]]| 創建=明治12年（1879年）| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 例祭=[[4月5日]]・[[10月5日]]}}
'''山梨縣護國神社'''（やまなしけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の英霊25039柱を祀る。
[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。
現鎮座地である岩窪は[[甲府藩]]主[[柳沢氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[黄檗宗]]寺院[[永慶寺]]の所在地で、永慶寺は享保9年の甲斐国一円が[[幕府直轄領]]化された際に大和郡山に移転された。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。

== 交通 == *[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[中央本線]][[甲府駅]]北口2番のりばより山梨交通バス「武田神社」行きまたは「積翠寺」行きの乗車し、「護国神社入口」バス停下車、徒歩約10分。

[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の英霊25039柱を祀る。
[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。
現鎮座地である岩窪は[[甲府藩]]主[[柳沢氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[黄檗宗]]寺院[[永慶寺]]の所在地で、永慶寺は享保9年の甲斐国一円が[[幕府直轄領]]化された際に大和郡山に移転された。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。
== 交通 == *[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[中央本線]][[甲府駅]]北口2番のりばより山梨交通バス「武田神社」行きまたは「積翠寺」行きに乗車し、「護国神社入口」バス停下車、徒歩約10分。
==外部リンク== *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 山梨縣護國神社]（山梨県神社庁）

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:13 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 山梨縣護國神社 |画像 = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|240px]] |所在地 = 山梨県甲府市岩窪町608 |ISO = |緯度度 = 35|緯度分=41|緯度秒=01 |経度度 = 138|経度分=34|経度秒=56 |ラベル位置 = |地図 = Japan Yamanashi |祭神 = 護国の[[英霊]] |神体 = |社格 = 内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治12年（1879年） |神殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月5日]]・[[10月5日]] |神事 = |アクセス = }}
'''山梨縣護國神社'''（やまなしけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の戦死者25039柱を祀る。
[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。
現鎮座地である岩窪は[[甲府藩]]主[[柳沢氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[黄檗宗]]寺院[[永慶寺]]の所在地で、永慶寺は享保9年の甲斐国一円が[[幕府直轄領]]化された際に大和郡山に移転された。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。
建物は、昭和17年（1942年）建築。主に全国護国神社設計を行った、内務省神社局の[[角南隆]]による設計プラン。護国神社は祭典に祭神の遺族、関係団体等の参列が非常に多いことから、拝殿前に広場を設けており「拝殿等の祭典を行う社殿建築は、この広場と連携して祭儀の際にも支障なく、平時に於いてもよく調和するものでなくてはならぬ」という考えに基づいている。
== 交通 == *[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[甲府駅]]北口2番のりばより[[山梨交通|山梨交通バス]]「武田神社」行きまたは「積翠寺」行きに乗車し、「護国神社入口」バス停下車、徒歩約10分。
<gallery> Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|公道から入口を見る（2017年8月17日撮影） </gallery>
==外部リンク== *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 山梨縣護國神社]（山梨県神社庁）

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20230422204826|u=Immanuelle|ns=118}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] }} [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:13 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 山梨縣護國神社 |画像 = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = [[山梨県]][[甲府市]]岩窪町608 |位置 = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 護国の[[英霊]] |神体 = |社格 = 内務大臣指定[[護国神社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建 = [[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]） |神殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月5日]]・[[10月5日]] |神事 = |アクセス = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=山梨縣
護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E}}}} }} '''山梨縣護國神社'''（やまなしけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。[[西南戦争]]以来の山梨県関係の戦没軍人・軍属の戦死者25039柱を祀る。 ==略歴== [[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）、[[招魂社]]として市内太田町に建立されたのに始まる。昭和17年（[[1942年]]）に現在地に遷座し、昭和19年（[[1944年]]）、山梨縣護國神社に改称した。戦後の一時期、山梨宮に改称していた。現在「山梨宮」は、山梨県出身の殉職自衛官などを祀る[[摂社]]の名前になっている。 現鎮座地である岩窪は[[甲府藩]]主[[柳沢氏]]の[[菩提寺]]である[[黄檗宗]]寺院[[永慶寺]]の所在地で、永慶寺は享保9年の甲斐国一円が[[幕府直轄領]]化された際に大和郡山に移転された。また、山梨県内有数の桜の名所としても知られている。 建物は、昭和17年（1942年）建築。主に全国護国神社設計を行った、内務省神社局の[[角南隆]]による設計プラン。護国神社は祭典に祭神の遺族、関係団体等の参列が非常に多いことから、拝殿前に広場を設けており「拝殿等の祭典を行う社殿建築は、この広場と連携して祭儀の際にも支障なく、平時に於いてもよく調和するものでなくてはならぬ」という考えに基づいている。 == 交通 == *[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[甲府駅]]北口2番のりばより[[山梨交通|山梨交通バス]]「武田神社」行きまたは「積翠寺」行きに乗車し、「護国神社入口」バス停下車、徒歩約10分。 ==ギャラリー== Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|公道から入口を見る（2017年8月17日撮影） Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|階段（2013年2月） Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|社殿（2013年2月） ==外部リンク== {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 山梨縣護國神社]（山梨県神社庁） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やまなしけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:山梨県の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:桜に関する場所]] [[Category:甲府市の建築物]]

It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:GokokuTest]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] }} [[Category:Original pages]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * {{AFC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}}
