Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{AFC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] == Gallery == Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) == External Links == (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{DEFAULTSORT:: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{DEFAULTSORT:: Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{AFC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kōfu]] [[Category:Cherry blossom spots in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu]] Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = {{ill|April 5|ja|4月5日}}, {{ill|October 5|ja|10月5日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]], [[Russo-Japanese War]], and [[World War II]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}}[[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] ==Japanese content== '''Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (山梨縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市|lt=Kōfu City}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, Japan. It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}} It enshrines 25,039 spirits of fallen military servicemen and civilian employees from Yamanashi Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Seinan War|ja|西南戦争|lt=Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)}} onward. == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of {{ill|Kōfu Station|ja|甲府駅|lt=Kōfu Station}} (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) {{citation needed| Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja}}
