revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AfC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Gokokujinja-shrine_Kofu-city_Yamanashi_Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG|250px]]
Torii and Haiden | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|1|N|138|34|56|E|display=inline,title}} | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}}{{Beppyo Shrines}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|山梨縣護國神社|version=104534951|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:山梨縣護國神社]] [[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] [[sn:amanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hanami spots of Japan]] [[Category:History of Kofu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Religion (Kōfu)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] {{Commons category|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[simple:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469919]] Wikidata name: Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AfC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|35.68361111|N|138.58222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.yamanashi-jinjacho.or.jp/intro/search/detail/1062 Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Yamanashi Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{shinto-stub}}{{Beppyo Shrines}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|山梨縣護國神社|version=104534951|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:山梨縣護國神社]] [[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]] [[sn:amanashi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] 2]] [[Category:Hanami spots of Japan]] [[Category:History of Kofu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Religion (Kōfu)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] {{Commons category|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]]Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469919]] Wikidata name: Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AfC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|35.68361111|N|138.58222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|35.68361111|N|138.58222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine [[sn:commons:Category:Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]][[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[simple:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469919]] Wikidata name: Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|35.68361111|N|138.58222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo [[sn:commons:Category:Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[de:Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein]][[ja:山梨縣護國神社]][[simple:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11469919]] Wikidata name: Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{AfC comment|1=This shrine does not appear notable enough to need its own page, separate from the Gokoku Shrines page. Your other shrine pages do seem to be of more notable shrines, but will need to include more sources to have pass AfC. [[User:Gorden 2211|Gorden 2211]] ([[User talk:Gorden 2211|talk]]) 01:48, 24 July 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto Shrine}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Gokokujinja-shrine Kofu-city Yamanashi Japan.JPG | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|35.68361111|N|138.58222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1879. | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to the courtyard of the facility Landesverteidigungsschrein3.JPG|One of the many explanatory panels Landesverteidigungsschrein4.JPG|Column at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|View at the entrance Landesverteidigungsschrein6.JPG|Information about the [[:en:General|general]] [[:en:Amari Torayasu|Amari Torayasu]], who is associated with this shrine Landesverteidigungsschrein7.JPG|Gatekeeper Landesverteidigungsschrein8.JPG|Explanation of the historical significance of the site Landesverteidigungsschrein9.JPG|A [[Torii]] gate as the entrance gate [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. from August 17, 2017) Landesverteidigungsschrein5.JPG|Stairs (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein1.JPG|Main shrine building (February 2013) Landesverteidigungsschrein2.JPG|The main entrance to Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja (Yamanashi Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Yamanashi | location = Iwakubo-chō 608, [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] | deity = Spirits of the war dead (eirei) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 12 (1879) | festival = [[:en:April 5|April 5]], [[:en:October 5|October 5]] | prefecture= {{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}} }} {{ill|Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|ja|山梨縣護國神社|1=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|de|Yamanashi-ken-Gokoku-Schrein|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (山梨縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]][[:en:Japan|Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku Shrine - Kōfu |url=http://wikimapia.org/49466/Yamanashi-Gokoku-Shrine |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=wikimapia.org |language=en}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Cite web |title=Yamanashi Gokoku-jinja Shrine｜Chichibu Okan > Tracing the major sites on Chichibu Okan Road {{!}} Yamanashi Historical road Tourism |url=https://rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp/en/spot/3-41.html |access-date=2023-04-22 |website=rekishinomichi-yamanashi.jp}} It is located 600 meters east of [[Takeda Shrine]]. It was founded in 1879. It enshrines 25,062 fallen soldiers from wars including the [[:en:Satsuma Rebellion|Seinan War (Satsuma Rebellion)]], [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], and [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]].Gokoku shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]..{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == Brief History == It was first established in Meiji 12 (1879) as a Shōkonsha (a shrine for inviting the spirits of the war dead) in Ōta-chō within the city. In Shōwa 17 (1942), it was relocated to its current site, and in Shōwa 19 (1944), it was renamed Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja. After World War II, for a time it was renamed “Yamanashimiya.” Currently, “Yamanashimiya” is the name of an associated shrine (sessha) that enshrines, among others, Yamanashi-born members of the Self-Defense Forces who died in the line of duty. The current location, Iwakubo, was the site of the Obaku sect Buddhist temple Eikeiji (永慶寺), the family temple (bodaiji) of the Yanagisawa clan who were lords of the Kōfu Domain. When the entire Kai Province came under direct Bakufu control in Kyōhō 9 (1724), Eikeiji was moved to Yamato Kōriyama. Yamanashi-ken Gokoku Jinja is also known as one of the premier cherry blossom viewing spots in the prefecture. The shrine buildings were constructed in Shōwa 17 (1942). The design was largely based on plans by Takashi Sunami, an official from the Ministry of the Interior’s Shrine Bureau, who was responsible for designing various gokoku shrines nationwide. Given that a large number of bereaved families and related groups would attend shrine rituals, a plaza was built in front of the Haiden. Sunami’s concept was that “the shrine building for rituals must harmonize with and function without hindrance during ceremonies, and also blend well during normal times” in coordination with the plaza. == Access == *From the north exit of [[:en:Kōfu Station|Kōfu Station]] (JR), take a Yamanashi Kōtsū Bus bound for either “Takeda Jinja” or “Sekisuiji” from bus stop #2. Get off at “Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi” (Gokoku Shrine Entrance) bus stop, then walk about 10 minutes. == Gallery == Yamanashiken-Gokoku-jinja-1.jpg|View from the public road toward the entrance (photo It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present.
